patterns.name: Missing Invisible Forces
patterns.what: Sometimes forgets to include invisible forces like magnetic force or friction when listing all the forces acting on an object.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Student correctly identified gravity and friction, but missed the magnetic force acting on the worksheet.
Found it tricky to identify all the true statements about the invisible forces acting on a thrown baseball.

patterns.strategic_advice: When a question asks about forces, always run through a mental checklist of invisible forces: **gravity**, **friction**, and **magnetism**. Ask yourself if any magnets are present or if things are rubbing together.

patterns.name: Fair Test Set-Ups
patterns.what: Occasionally struggles to pick the right experimental setups where only one variable changes and everything else stays exactly the same.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Needed to test the number of batteries, but didn't choose the two setups that kept the number of coils exactly the same.
Found it tricky to isolate the changed variable while keeping all other conditions constant.

patterns.strategic_advice:
When you see prompts about experiments, treat them as a matching game:
- Highlight the **ONE thing** the experiment is testing (e.g., number of batteries).
- Make sure **every other detail** in the setups you choose is identical.

patterns.name: Following Energy Paths
patterns.what: Tends to get tripped up when having to follow the exact path of light bouncing off mirrors or electricity flowing through a circuit.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Found it challenging to trace the closed loops in a circuit to see which switch would light up the most bulbs.
Needed to follow the straight lines of light reflecting off mirrors to see which viewing tube worked.
Struggled to visualize how opening or closing specific switches affects the flow of current to each bulb.

patterns.strategic_advice: Use your pencil! When dealing with circuits or light, physically **draw the path** on the paper. For circuits, trace from the battery's positive to negative end. For light, draw straight lines bouncing off mirrors.

patterns.name: Reading Line Graphs
patterns.what: Sometimes misinterprets line graphs when trying to figure out how a changing environment affects animal populations.

specific_examples.whatWentWrong: Found it tricky to match the depth of water to the amount of light, and then to the population size.
Struggled to read the graph to see which organism's population goes up when light increases.

patterns.strategic_advice: When looking at a graph, always read the labels on the bottom and the side first. If the question mentions a hidden factor like **'amount of light'**, write it directly onto the graph next to the depth numbers so you don't forget the relationship.
